concerns.text: The chi‑squared goodness‑of‑fit test is described as comparing observed inconsistency counts to a “uniform‑error null hypothesis”. This null hypothesis is not standard for assessing prevalence of reporting errors and is not justified in the specification; the test may therefore be inappropriate for the research question.

Flagging a summary as inconsistent when the absolute difference between reported and reconstructed p‑values exceeds 0.05 uses raw p‑value differences, which are not on a linear scale. A 0.05 difference near p = 0 is far more consequential than the same difference near p = 0.5, potentially inflating false‑positive inconsistency rates.
